title: Trauma and the Breath: Why Trauma Survivors Hold Their Breath
excerpt: Discover why trauma survivors often hold their breath and how understanding breath can unlock healing from trauma.
body:
Introduction: The Breath as Trauma’s Silent Witness

Breath is our most basic life force, yet it often goes unnoticed—until trauma intervenes. For many trauma survivors, breathing is not just automatic; it becomes a battleground. Why do so many hold their breath unconsciously? What does this mean for healing? This article explores the science and somatic wisdom behind trauma and breath, pulling from leading researchers like Bessel van der Kolk, Stephen Porges, and Peter Levine to decode why trauma survivors often hold their breath and how breathwork can be a powerful tool in trauma recovery.

Understanding Breath: More Than Oxygen Exchange

Breathing is often taken for granted. It’s an automatic process controlled by the brainstem—no conscious effort needed. But breathing also reflects our emotional and physiological states. Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory highlights how the autonomic nervous system (ANS) regulates breath in response to safety or threat cues.

When we feel safe, our breath tends to be deep, slow, and even. In contrast, when we perceive danger, the breath becomes shallow, rapid, or irregular. For trauma survivors, the nervous system frequently remains stuck in a defensive state, causing altered breathing patterns that can become chronic.

Why Trauma Survivors Hold Their Breath

Holding the breath is more than just a habit; it’s a physiological survival strategy. According to Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, trauma triggers a freeze or immobilization response, akin to what animals exhibit when facing inescapable threat. During this freeze state, breath often becomes shallow or suspended.

Bessel van der Kolk explains in The Body Keeps the Score that trauma is stored in the body and manifests as chronic tension, constriction, and breath-holding. The breath literally gets caught as the body attempts to shut down overwhelming sensations or memories.

“Trauma survivors often breathe in a way that reflects their nervous system’s ongoing state of alert or shutdown. Holding the breath is a way the body tries to protect itself from re-experiencing pain or danger.” – The Oracle Lover

Freeze, Flight, Fight—and Breath

Traditional trauma response models focus on fight, flight, and freeze. While fight and flight mobilize energy, freeze immobilizes it. Breath plays a key role here:

  • Fight/Flight: Breath quickens to fuel action.
  • Freeze: Breath slows or stops to minimize movement and reduce detection.

When trauma triggers freeze, breath-holding can become habitual, interfering with natural respiration and nervous system regulation.

The Nervous System’s Role in Breath Patterns After Trauma

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory illuminates how the vagus nerve modulates the heart and lungs to regulate our state of safety or threat. Trauma can dysregulate this system, leading to chronic sympathetic (fight/flight) or dorsal vagal (shutdown) dominance.

In dorsal vagal states, breath is often held or very shallow, reflecting a shutdown or immobilization. This breath-holding is a somatic marker of dissociation and disconnection from the body. On the flip side, sympathetic overdrive causes rapid, shallow breathing, often experienced as anxiety or panic.

How Breath-Holding Impacts Trauma Recovery

Breath-holding isn’t just an annoying symptom—it actively maintains trauma’s grip. When breath is restricted, the body’s ability to regulate the autonomic nervous system is compromised. This perpetuates hyperarousal or numbing, making it harder to access calm, grounded states.

Deb Dana, a leading therapist applying Polyvagal Theory clinically, emphasizes that restoring natural breath rhythms is key to reclaiming safety and social engagement. Breathwork can help re-tune the nervous system and build resilience.

Somatic Approaches to Releasing Breath-Holding

Somatic healing methods focus on bringing awareness and gentle movement back to the breath to help trauma survivors reconnect with their bodies and nervous systems.

  • Somatic Experiencing (SE): Developed by Peter Levine, SE uses tracking bodily sensations, including breath, to discharge trapped energy and renegotiate trauma responses.
  • Polyvagal-informed Breathwork: Deb Dana suggests slow, patterned breathing to engage the ventral vagal system, promoting safety and social connection.
  • Mindful Breath Awareness: Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) encourages noticing breath without judgment, helping survivors differentiate parts of themselves holding trauma.
  • EMDR and Breath: Francine Shapiro’s EMDR therapy often pairs eye movements with controlled breathing to process traumatic memories while maintaining regulation.

Practical Breath Exercises for Trauma Survivors

While breathwork can be powerful, it must be approached gently and with safety in mind. Here are some accessible exercises informed by trauma research:

  • Resonant Breathing: Breathe slowly in and out, aiming for about 5-6 breaths per minute. This stimulates the ventral vagal pathway, promoting calm.
  • Box Breathing: Inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 4 seconds, exhale for 4 seconds, hold for 4 seconds. This rhythm helps regain control over breath and nervous system.
  • Sighing Out: A deep inhale followed by a slow, audible exhale can release tension and trapped energy.
  • Gentle Breath Tracking: Simply notice the breath without trying to change it, cultivating mindfulness and curiosity.

Always practice breathwork in a safe space and consider working with a trauma-informed therapist or somatic coach to guide you.

Conclusion: Breath as a Gateway to Healing

Trauma survivors hold their breath not just metaphorically but physiologically. This breath-holding is a survival adaptation that, over time, can trap them in cycles of dysregulation and distress. Understanding the nervous system’s role in breath and trauma reveals why reconnecting with breath is essential to healing.

Grounded in science and somatic wisdom, trauma-informed breathwork offers a path to reclaiming safety, presence, and vitality. As Gabor Maté reminds us, healing trauma means listening to the body’s signals—including the breath—and responding with compassion and care.

Breath is not just oxygen; it is the thread that weaves body and mind back together after trauma.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Penguin Books.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Maté, G. (2018). When the Body Says No: The Cost of Hidden Stress. Wiley.
  • Shapiro, F. (2017). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy, Third Edition: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Publications.
  • Schwartz, R. C. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: Why do trauma survivors often hold their breath?
faqs.a: Holding the breath is a physiological survival response linked to the freeze state in trauma. It helps the body minimize movement and sensory input during overwhelming threat.

faqs.q: How does breath affect the nervous system after trauma?
faqs.a: Breath patterns influence autonomic nervous system regulation. Trauma can cause breath-holding or shallow breathing, which keeps the nervous system in a state of threat or shutdown.

faqs.q: Can breathwork really help with trauma recovery?
faqs.a: Yes. Trauma-informed breathwork helps restore natural breathing rhythms, regulates the nervous system, and supports reconnecting with the body safely.

faqs.q: Is it safe to practice breath exercises on my own if I have trauma?
faqs.a: Start gently and listen to your body. It’s best to work with a trauma-informed therapist or somatic practitioner to guide breathwork safely, especially if you have intense symptoms.

affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that reveals how trauma is stored in the body and offers insights into healing through body-based approaches.

affiliateProducts.title: In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: Explains the physiology of trauma and offers somatic techniques to release trauma through awareness of the body and breath.

affiliateProducts.title: The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: A practical guide for therapists and individuals to understand and apply Polyvagal Theory for nervous system regulation.
